steps.title: A Day in Korean Life
steps.desc:
The verbs you use every single day.

From waking up to going to sleep — the complete daily routine vocabulary.
goals: Build a natural daily schedule in Korean

steps.exampleEn: I wake up at 7 every day.
steps.note:
일어나다: to get up/wake up.
일어나 + 아요 = 일어나요 (present).
일어나 + 았어요 = 일어났어요 (past).

매일: every day.
7시에: at 7 o'clock.

일찍 일어나요: wake up early.
늦게 일어나요: wake up late.

steps.exampleEn: After waking up, I wash.
steps.note:
씻다: to wash.
씻 + 어요 = 씻어요 (present).
씻 + 었어요 = 씻었어요 (past).

세수하다: to wash face (세수 + 하다).
샤워하다: to shower.

일어나고 나서 씻어요.
= After getting up, I wash.

steps.en: to go to work / to leave work
steps.exampleEn: I go to work at 9. I leave work at 6.
steps.note:
출근하다: to go to work.
출 (出: go out) + 근 (勤: work/attendance).

퇴근하다: to leave work.
퇴 (退: withdraw) + 근 (勤: work).

출근 시간: commute time.
퇴근 후: after work.

출근했어요: went to work.
퇴근했어요: left work.

steps.exampleEn: I work at a company.
steps.note:
일하다: to work.
일 (work) + 하다 (do).

회사 (會社): company.
회 (meet/gather) + 사 (company/society).

회사에서 일해요: I work at a company.
오늘 많이 일했어요: I worked a lot today.

steps.exampleEn: I usually sleep at 11.
steps.note:
자다: to sleep.
자 + 아요 = 자요 (ㅏ+ㅏ merge).
자 + 았어요 = 잤어요.

일찍 자요: sleep early.
늦게 자요: sleep late.
잘 잤어요?: Did you sleep well?

steps.title: 🌅 A Complete Day
steps.text:
아침: 일어나다 → 씻다 → 아침 먹다
Morning: wake up → wash → eat breakfast

오전: 출근하다 → 일하다
Morning: go to work → work

점심: 점심 먹다
Lunch: eat lunch

오후: 일하다 → 퇴근하다
Afternoon: work → leave work

저녁: 저녁 먹다 → 쉬다 → 자다
Evening: eat dinner → rest → sleep

연결:
일어나서 씻어요.
씻고 나서 밥을 먹어요.
밥을 먹고 출근해요.
퇴근하고 나서 운동해요.
운동하고 밥을 먹어요.
밥을 먹고 쉬어요.

steps.hint: 출 (出) = go out. 출근 = go out + attendance = go to work.
퇴 (退) = withdraw. 퇴근 = withdraw from work = leave work.
자다 = sleep. 잤어요 = slept. 잘 잤어요? = slept well?
Wake up → wash → go to work.

steps.s: 매일 7시에 ___. (I wake up at 7 every day.)
steps.hint: 일어나다 = wake up. Present: 일어나요.

steps.s: 6시에 ___하고 집에 가요. (I leave work at 6 and go home.)
steps.hint: 퇴근하다 = leave work.

steps.s: 일어나___ 나서 씻어요. (After waking up, I wash.)
steps.hint: -고 나서 = after doing.

steps.s: 7시에 {1}. 씻{2} 나서 아침을 {3}. 9시에 {4}해요.
steps.hint: Wake up at 7. After washing, eat breakfast. Go to work at 9.
회 (會) = meet/gather. 회사 = gathering place = company.
세수 (洗手: wash + hand/face) + 하다.

steps.title: Home Vocabulary
steps.desc: Rooms, furniture, and the spaces of Korean daily life.
goals: Learn room names: 방, 부엌, 화장실, 거실, 침실
Understand compound structure of home words
Prepare for 전세/월세 culture in Lesson 6

steps.exampleEn: My room is small.
steps.note:
방 (房): room.

The most versatile word in Korean housing:
방: room (general)

Compounds with 방:
노래방: singing room (karaoke)
피시방: PC room (gaming café)
찜질방: steam room (bathhouse)
만화방: comic room (manga café)

방 culture is huge in Korea.

steps.exampleEn: I cook in the kitchen.
steps.note:
부엌: kitchen (pure Korean).
화장실: bathroom/restroom.
화장 (化粧: makeup/grooming) + 실 (室: room).

거실: living room.
거 (居: reside) + 실 (室: room).

침실: bedroom.
침 (寢: sleep) + 실 (室: room).

steps.exampleEn: I take off shoes at the entrance.
steps.note:
현관 (玄關): entrance/entryway.

⚠️ Korean culture: shoes OFF at the door.
현관에서 신발을 벗어요.
벗다: to take off (clothing/shoes).
신발: shoes.

욕실 (浴室): bathing room.
욕 (bath) + 실 (room).

화장실 vs 욕실:
화장실: toilet/restroom (general).
욕실: bathroom with bathtub/shower.

steps.exampleEn: When you enter the house, please take off your shoes.
steps.note:
신발을 벗다: take off shoes.

This is CRITICAL Korean culture.
Always remove shoes when entering:
• Someone's home
• Some restaurants (especially traditional)
• Temples

슬리퍼: slippers (provided inside).

집에 들어오면 신발을 벗으세요.
들어오다: to come in/enter.
들어오면: if/when you enter.

steps.title: 🏡 Korean Housing Types
steps.text:
원룸: studio apartment (one room)
오피스텔: small apartment/office hybrid
아파트: apartment (large complex)
빌라: villa/low-rise apartment
주택: house

Korean apartments (아파트) are the most common housing.
Large complexes with 20-30 floors.

전세/월세: unique Korean rental systems (Lesson 6).

한국 집 특징 (Korean home features):
• 온돌: underfloor heating (traditional)
• 현관: shoe removal area
• 베란다: enclosed balcony (for laundry)
• 김치냉장고: kimchi refrigerator

steps.hint: 실 (室) = room. 화장실, 침실, 거실, 욕실 — all rooms.

steps.q: When entering a Korean home, you should:
opts: Take off your shoes at 현관
steps.ans: Take off your shoes at 현관
steps.hint: Shoes off at the entrance. Always.
침 (寢) = sleep. 침실 = sleep room = bedroom.
거 (居) = reside/live. 거실 = residing room = living room.

steps.q: Which is NOT a real Korean '방'?
steps.hint: 노래방 (karaoke), 피시방 (PC café), 찜질방 (bathhouse) are real. 운동방 doesn't exist — gyms are 헬스장.

steps.s: ___에서 요리해요. (I cook in the kitchen.)
steps.hint: 부엌 = kitchen.

steps.s: 집에 들어오면 신발을 ___. (When entering, take off shoes.)
steps.hint: 벗다 = take off. 벗으세요 = please take off (polite request).

steps.s: {1}에서 요리하고, {2}에서 텔레비전을 봐요. {3}에서 자요.
steps.hint: Cook in the kitchen, watch TV in the living room, sleep in the bedroom.
온돌 = traditional Korean underfloor heating system.
원 (one) + 룸 (room) = studio apartment.

steps.s: ___에서 텔레비전을 봐요. (I watch TV in the living room.)
steps.hint: 거실 = living room.

steps.exampleEn: I sit on the chair. I sleep in bed.
steps.note:
가구 (furniture):

의자 (椅子): chair. 의자에 앉다: sit on a chair.
침대 (寢臺): bed. 침 (sleep) + 대 (platform).
창문 (窓門): window. 창 (window) + 문 (door).

의자가 편해요: the chair is comfortable.
침대가 커요: the bed is big.
창문을 열어요: I open the window.

steps.hint: 침 (寢) = sleep. 대 (臺) = platform. Sleeping platform = bed.

steps.title: Casual Speech — 반말
steps.desc:
You've been hearing about 반말 since Unit 3 Lesson 7b.
'It exists. Don't use it yet.'

Now you're ready.

반말 is the speech level for close friends, siblings, children, and people younger than you.

This is a P26 harvest. You already know the feeling. Now you own the rules.

steps.title: 🤝 You've Felt This Since Unit 3
steps.text:
Unit 3 Lesson 7b seeded 반말:
'There's a casual level. Close friends use it. Don't use it yet.'

Since then, you've heard it in cultural notes and dialogues.
You know 해요체 deeply. 반말 is built from it.

THE BASIC RULE:
해요체 → 반말: just drop -요.

먹어요 → 먹어
가요 → 가
좋아요 → 좋아
했어요 → 했어
갈 거예요 → 갈 거야

That's it. 반말 = 해요체 minus -요.
(With one special case: 이에요/예요 → 이야/야)

steps.exampleEn: eat (polite) → eat (casual)
steps.note:
Present:
먹어요 → 먹어
가요 → 가
와요 → 와
해요 → 해
좋아요 → 좋아
있어요 → 있어

Past:
먹었어요 → 먹었어
갔어요 → 갔어
했어요 → 했어

Future:
먹을 거예요 → 먹을 거야
갈 거예요 → 갈 거야
할 거예요 → 할 거야

⚠️ Future 반말: 거예요 → 거야 (not just dropping 요).
것이야 contracts to 거야.

steps.exampleEn: is a student (polite → casual)
steps.note:
이다 (to be) in 반말:

Consonant ending + 이야: 학생이야 (is a student)
Vowel ending + 야: 뭐야? (what is it?)

이에요 → 이야
예요 → 야

이거 뭐야?: What's this? (casual)
나 학생이야: I'm a student. (casual)

⚠️ In 반말, 저 (I, polite) becomes 나 (I, casual).
저는 → 나는 / 난

steps.exampleEn: I'm a student. And you?
steps.note:
반말 pronouns:

저 → 나: I (casual)
저는 → 나는 / 난: I (topic)
저를 → 나를 / 날: me (object)

당신 is NOT used for 'you' in Korean!
With 반말, use 너: you (casual).
너는 / 넌: you (topic)
너를 / 널: you (object)

⚠️ 너 is ONLY for 반말 relationships.
Never use 너 with strangers or elders.

steps.example: 어디 가? 뭐 해? 밥 먹었어?
steps.exampleEn: Where are you going? What are you doing? Did you eat?
steps.note:
반말 questions — just drop -요 and raise intonation:

어디 가요? → 어디 가?
뭐 해요? → 뭐 해?
밥 먹었어요? → 밥 먹었어?
이거 뭐예요? → 이거 뭐야?

밥 먹었어? = Did you eat?
This is the most common Korean casual greeting.
Literally asking about food = caring about you.

steps.title: ⚠️ 반말 Social Rules — CRITICAL
steps.text:
반말 is NOT about being rude. It's about CLOSENESS.

Use 반말 with:
✅ Close friends your age or younger
✅ Children
✅ Close siblings
✅ Your partner (after a while)

NEVER use 반말 with:
❌ Strangers (even same age)
❌ Anyone older than you (unless they ask)
❌ Work superiors
❌ Service staff
❌ People you just met

Switching to 반말:
Koreans explicitly negotiate this:
우리 말 놓을까요? = Shall we speak casually?
말 놓다: to 'drop' formal speech.

Using 반말 without permission = RUDE.

The golden rule: when in doubt, use 해요체.

deepDive.title: 반말 and 나이 (Age)
deepDive.text:
Korean social hierarchy is built on age.

Koreans often ask age when meeting:
몇 살이에요? / 나이가 어떻게 되세요?

This isn't rude — it determines speech level.

Same age (동갑): 반말 often happens quickly.
1-2 years older: may use 해요체 or negotiate 반말.
3+ years older: 해요체 or 존댓말 is expected.

형/오빠 (older brother): 반말 TO you is normal.
동생 (younger sibling): you can use 반말 TO them.

This system feels rigid to foreigners.
But for Koreans, it creates comfort and clarity.

steps.hint: 반말 = drop -요. 먹어요 → 먹어.
Future 반말: 거예요 → 거야. 갈 거야.
이에요 → 이야. 학생이야.
저 (polite I) → 나 (casual I).

opts: With close friends same age or younger
steps.ans: With close friends same age or younger
steps.hint: 반말 = closeness. Only with close friends, siblings, children.
말 놓다 = drop formal speech = switch to 반말.

steps.s: 뭐 ___? (What are you doing? — casual)
steps.hint: 뭐 해요? → 뭐 해? (반말 = drop -요)

steps.s: 밥 ___? (Did you eat? — casual)
steps.hint: 밥 먹었어요? → 밥 먹었어? (drop -요)

steps.s: 이거 ___? (What is this? — casual)
steps.hint: 뭐예요? → 뭐야? (예요 → 야)

steps.s: A: 어디 {1}? B: 학교에 {2}. A: {3} 먹었어? B: 응, 먹었어.
steps.hint: A: Where are you going? B: To school. A: Did you eat? B: Yeah, I ate. (All 반말)

steps.q: Which is the polite response to an elder: 네 or 응?
steps.hint: 네 = polite yes. 응 = casual yes (반말). Use 네 with elders.

steps.title: Convenience Store Life
steps.desc:
The Korean 편의점 is not just a store — it's a lifeline.

Open 24/7, they sell everything from meals to concert tickets.
goals: Cultural context: why 편의점 matters in Korea

steps.exampleEn: I bought a lunchbox at the convenience store.
steps.note:
편의점 (便宜店): convenience store.
편의 (convenience) + 점 (店: shop/store).

Major chains: CU, GS25, 세븐일레븐, 이마트24.

편의점 in Korea:
• Every corner — over 50,000 nationwide
• Open 24/7
• Food, drinks, ATM, printing, delivery pickup
• Common meal spot for workers and students

steps.exampleEn: Two triangle kimbaps please.
steps.note:
도시락: lunchbox/bento.
삼각김밥: triangle-shaped kimbap (onigiri style).
삼각 (三角): triangle. 김밥: seaweed rice roll.

~1,000–2,000원 each.

Other popular 편의점 foods:
컵라면: cup ramen
샌드위치: sandwich
핫바: hot bar (meat stick)

steps.exampleEn: I bought water at the convenience store.
steps.note:
사다: to buy.
사 + 았어요 = 샀어요 (past).

뭐 샀어요? = What did you buy?
이거 어디에서 샀어요? = Where did you buy this?

사다 is bright vowel ㅏ → 았어요.
사 + 았 = 샀어요 (ㅏ+ㅏ contraction).

steps.exampleEn: How much is this?
steps.note:
얼마예요?: how much?

이거 얼마예요? = How much is this?

Answers:
천 원이에요. = It's 1,000 won.
이천오백 원이에요. = It's 2,500 won.

~1,000원: triangle kimbap.
~4,000원: cup ramen.
~1,500원: bottle of water.

steps.title: 🏪 편의점 Culture
steps.text:
Korean 편의점 is a social institution:

• Students study there (free WiFi, tables)
• Workers eat lunch there (cheap, fast)
• Night owls go there at 3am (always open)
• You can pay bills, print documents, pick up packages

Phrases:
봉투 필요하세요?: Do you need a bag?
데워 드릴까요?: Shall I heat it up?
카드로 할게요: I'll pay by card.

편의점 sitting culture:
Many have indoor/outdoor seating.
컵라면 + hot water machine = cheap meal.

편의점 in Korean life = 빨리빨리 culture.
Fast, convenient, always there.

deepDive.title: 편의점 Vocabulary
deepDive.text:
봉투: plastic bag
영수증: receipt
데우다: to heat up
전자레인지: microwave
충전하다: to charge (phone/T-money)
택배: delivery package

계산할게요: I'll check out.
카드로 할게요: I'll pay by card.
현금으로 할게요: I'll pay in cash.

현금 (現金): cash.
현 (current) + 금 (gold/money).

steps.hint: 점 (店) = store/shop. 편의점 = convenience + store.
삼 (3) + 각 (angle) = triangle.
데우다 = heat up. 드릴까요 = shall I (for you)?
사: ㅏ + ㅏ merge. 사+았어요 = 샀어요.

steps.s: 이거 ___예요? (How much is this?)
steps.hint: 얼마 = how much. 얼마예요? = how much is it?

steps.s: 편의점에서 삼각김밥을 ___. (I bought triangle kimbap at the convenience store.)
steps.hint: 사다 → 샀어요 (past).

steps.s: 편의점에서 {1}을 {2}. {3}예요? 천오백 원이에요.
steps.hint: I bought triangle kimbap at the convenience store. How much is it? 1,500 won.
카드로 = by card. 할게요 = I'll do.

steps.s: ___에서 컵라면을 샀어요. (I bought cup ramen at the convenience store.)
steps.hint: 편의점 = convenience store.

opts: Do you want to heat it?
steps.hint: 봉투 = bag. 필요하다 = to need.
Major Korean 편의점 chains.

steps.title: Delivery Culture — Korea's Superpower
steps.desc:
Korea has the most advanced delivery culture in the world.

배달 (delivery) is not just pizza — it's everything, everywhere, fast.
goals: Know 치킨 as a cultural institution

steps.exampleEn: Let's order delivery.
steps.note:
배달 (配達): delivery.
배 (distribute) + 달 (reach/deliver).

배달 시키다: to order delivery.
시키다: to order/make someone do.

배달 시켜요! = Let's order delivery!
배달 시켰어요 = I ordered delivery.

steps.exampleEn: How much is the delivery fee?
steps.note:
배달앱: delivery app.
배달 + 앱 (app).

Major apps: 배달의민족 (Baedal Minjok), 쿠팡이츠, 요기요.

배달비: delivery fee.
배달 + 비 (費: fee/cost).

배달비가 얼마예요? = How much is the delivery fee?
Minimum order: 최소 주문 금액.

steps.exampleEn: Shall we order chicken?
steps.note:
치킨: Korean fried chicken.

Not just chicken — a cultural institution.
치킨 + 맥주 = 치맥 (chicken + beer).

Chicken delivery is THE most popular 배달 in Korea.

Popular chains: 교촌치킨, BBQ, BHC, 굽네치킨.

치킨 시킬까요? = Shall we order chicken?
양념치킨: seasoned chicken.
후라이드: fried (plain) chicken.
반반: half and half (양념 + 후라이드).

steps.en: please leave it at the door
steps.exampleEn: Please leave it at the door.
steps.note:
문 앞에 놔 주세요:
문 (door) + 앞에 (in front of) + 놓다 (put) + 주세요 (please do for me).

놓다 → 놔 (casual form of 놓아).

This is the standard contactless delivery phrase.

다른 요청:
빨리 와 주세요: please come quickly.
젓가락 두 개 주세요: two chopsticks please.
수저 주세요: spoon and chopsticks please.

steps.title: 🛵 배달 Culture in Korea
steps.text:
Korean delivery is FAST:
• Chicken: 30-40 minutes
• Food: 20-30 minutes
• Groceries (쿠팡): same day or next morning
• Rocket fresh: delivered to your door before dawn

You can deliver to:
• Your home
• The park (한강 치킨!)
• Your office
• A hotel room
• Basically anywhere

배달의민족 (Baemin):
The biggest delivery app.
배달 + 의 (of) + 민족 (nation) = 'Delivery Nation.'

배달 tip culture:
Tipping is NOT common in Korea.
Delivery fee covers everything.

steps.hint: 시키다 = to order/make someone do. 배달 시키다 = order delivery.
치맥 = chicken + beer. Korean institution.

opts: Please leave it at the door
steps.ans: Please leave it at the door
steps.hint: 문 앞에 = in front of the door. 놔 주세요 = please put/leave.
비 (費) = fee/cost. 배달비 = delivery fee.

steps.s: 치킨 ___까요? (Shall we order chicken?)
steps.hint: 시키다 → -(으)ㄹ까요? 시킬까요?

steps.s: ___ 앞에 놔 주세요. (Please leave it at the door.)
steps.hint: 문 = door. 문 앞에 = in front of the door.

steps.s: {1} 시킬까요? {2}앱으로 주문할게요. {3} 앞에 놔 주세요.
steps.hint: Shall we order chicken? I'll order via delivery app. Please leave it at the door.

steps.q: Is tipping common for delivery in Korea?
steps.hint: Korea: no tipping culture. Delivery fee is the total cost.

steps.q: 배달의민족 = 배달 + 의 + 민족. 민족 means:
steps.hint: 민족 (民族) = nation/people. 배달의민족 = delivery nation.

steps.s: 양념치킨 아니면 후라이드? ___로 할까요? (Seasoned or fried? Shall we go half-half?)
steps.hint: 반반 = half and half.
주문 (注文) = order. 주문하다 = to order.

steps.title: Practical Survival Korean
steps.desc:
Bank, post office, laundry — the everyday errands vocabulary.

Plus: 전세 and 월세, Korea's unique rental system.
goals: Learn bank and post office vocabulary

steps.exampleEn: I need to go to the bank.
steps.note:
은행 (銀行): bank.
은 (銀: silver) + 행 (行: business/go).

은행에 가다: go to the bank.
계좌: bank account (計座: counting + seat).
송금하다: to transfer money.
인출하다: to withdraw.
ATM: 현금인출기.

steps.exampleEn: I sent a package at the post office.
steps.note:
우체국 (郵遞局): post office.
우 (mail) + 체 (deliver) + 국 (bureau).

소포: package/parcel.
편지: letter.
우표: stamp.
보내다: to send.

소포를 보냈어요: sent a package.

steps.exampleEn: I left clothes at the dry cleaners.
steps.note:
세탁소 (洗濯所): laundry/dry cleaners.
세탁 (washing) + 소 (place).

코인 세탁: coin laundry.
세탁기: washing machine.
맡기다: to entrust/leave with.

세탁소에 옷을 맡겼어요.
= I left clothes at the dry cleaners.

steps.exampleEn: Jeonse is expensive so I live with monthly rent.
steps.note:
전세 (傳貰): large lump-sum deposit rental.
전 (전체: whole) + 세 (rent).

Pay a HUGE deposit (often 50-80% of property value).
Live rent-free. Get deposit back when you leave.

월세 (月貰): monthly rent.
월 (month) + 세 (rent).
Smaller deposit + monthly payment.

This system is UNIQUE to Korea.
전세 is disappearing but still very common.

steps.title: 🏠 Korean Rental System
steps.text:
전세 (jeonse):
• Deposit: ₩100,000,000–500,000,000+ (huge!)
• Monthly rent: ₩0 (none)
• Landlord invests your deposit, earns interest
• You get ALL your money back when you leave
• Disappearing as interest rates change

월세 (wolse):
• Deposit: ₩5,000,000–30,000,000 (smaller)
• Monthly rent: ₩500,000–1,500,000
• More like Western renting
• Becoming more common

보증금: security deposit.
계약: contract.
집주인: landlord (집 + 주인: house + owner).
부동산: real estate agency.

부동산에 가다 = go to the real estate agency
(How you find an apartment in Korea)

steps.hint: 은 (銀) = silver. 은행 = silver business = bank.
전세: huge deposit, zero rent. Get deposit back when you leave.

steps.q: 우체국 = 우 + 체 + 국. 국 means:
steps.hint: 국 (局) = bureau. 우체국 = mail delivery bureau.
월 (月) = month. 월세 = monthly rent.

steps.s: ___에서 소포를 보냈어요. (I sent a package at the post office.)
steps.hint: 우체국 = post office.

steps.s: 전세가 비싸서 ___로 살아요. (Jeonse is expensive so I live with monthly rent.)
steps.hint: 월세 = monthly rent.

steps.s: {1}에 가서 돈을 인출했어요. {2}에서 소포를 보냈어요. {3}에서 옷을 맡겼어요.
steps.hint: Went to bank and withdrew money. Sent package at post office. Left clothes at dry cleaners.
부동산 = real estate (agency). Where you find apartments.
보증금 = security deposit.

steps.s: ___에 가서 계좌를 만들었어요. (I went to the bank and opened an account.)
steps.hint: 은행 = bank. 계좌 = account.
주인 = owner. 집주인 = house owner = landlord.

steps.exampleEn: I have a question. Please answer.
steps.note:
문제 (問題): problem/question (on a test).
문 (ask) + 제 (topic). 시험 문제: exam question.

질문 (質問): question (spoken).
질 (quality/nature) + 문 (ask).
질문 있어요?: Do you have a question?

대답 (對答): answer/reply.
대 (face/toward) + 답 (answer).
대답하다: to answer.

문제 = test question / problem.
질문 = spoken question.

steps.exampleEn: It's already 12! It's already finished.
steps.note:
벌써: already (surprise/time passed fast).
벌써 12시예요!: It's already 12! (Wow, time flew!)

이미: already (fact completed).
이미 끝났어요: It's already finished. (Fact.)

벌써: emotional (surprise at time).
이미: factual (the deed is done).

steps.hint: 질문 = question (spoken). 있어요 = have/exist.

steps.title: Telling Your Day — Extended Narration
steps.desc:
Connect everything you've learned into paragraph-length Korean.

You'll practice producing connected narratives about daily life.

Plus some new patterns you'll just notice — seeds for future units.
goals: Use all tenses, connectors, and vocabulary
Build confidence in extended Korean production

steps.exampleEn: Today I woke up early in the morning and exercised.
steps.note:
Narration building blocks:

Time: 오늘 아침에 (this morning)
Action: 일찍 일어나서 (woke up early and)
Result: 운동했어요 (exercised)

Connect with:
-고: and
-아/어서: because/and then
-고 나서: after doing
그래서: so
그런데: but

Each sentence should have TIME → PLACE → ACTION.

steps.exampleEn: It was harder than I expected.
steps.note:
생각보다: more than (I) thought.
생각 (thought/thinking) + 보다 (than).

생각보다 어려웠어요: harder than expected.
생각보다 재미있었어요: more fun than expected.
생각보다 쉬웠어요: easier than expected.

보다 is the comparison particle:
A보다 B: B more than A.
생각보다: more than thought.

steps.en: it was harder than expected (I found out)
steps.exampleEn: Korean was harder than I expected (I realized).
steps.note:
어렵더라고요.

-더라고요: 'I found out that...' / 'It turned out that...'

This ending shares a PERSONAL EXPERIENCE/DISCOVERY.

생각보다 어렵더라고요.
= I found out it was harder than expected.
(I'm telling you what I personally experienced.)

⚡ JRTF: -더라고요 is formally named in Unit 19.
For now: just hear it.
It's incredibly common in spoken Korean.

steps.en: I used to live there (but not anymore)
steps.exampleEn: I used to live in Seoul (but not anymore).
steps.note:
살았었어요.

-았/었었어요: DOUBLE PAST.
살았어요: lived (there)
살았었어요: USED TO live (there, but not anymore)

The extra 었 adds distance — 'that was then.'

예전에: in the past / before.

⚡ JRTF: -았/었었다 is formally named in Unit 17.
For now: just notice the difference.
살았어요 = lived
살았었어요 = used to live (not anymore)

steps.title: 📖 Sample: 나의 하루
steps.text:
오늘 7시에 일어나서 씻었어요.
씻고 나서 아침을 먹었어요.
9시에 출근했어요.
회사에서 많이 일했어요. 생각보다 바빴어요.
점심에 편의점에서 도시락을 샀어요.
6시에 퇴근하고 집에 갔어요.
집에서 치킨을 시켰어요.
치킨을 먹고 한국어를 공부했어요.
11시에 잤어요.

Notice:
• Time words start each sentence
• Connectors (-서, -고, -고 나서) link events
• Past tense throughout
• Natural, flowing narration

steps.hint: 생각 (thought) + 보다 (than) = more than expected.
-더라고요 = I found out that / it turned out that.
Double past -았었- adds distance: 'that was then, not now.'
보다 = than. A보다 B = B more than A.

steps.s: 한국어가 생각___ 재미있었어요. (Korean was more fun than expected.)
steps.hint: 생각보다 = more than expected.

steps.s: 예전에 부산에 ___. 지금은 서울에 살아요. (I used to live in Busan.)
steps.hint: Used to (not anymore) = double past: 살았었어요.

steps.s: 오늘 아침에 일찍 일어나{1} 운동했어요. 생각{2} 힘들었어요. 운동하고 {3} 아침을 먹었어요.
steps.hint: Woke up early this morning and exercised. It was harder than expected. After exercising, ate breakfast.
⚡ JRTF: -더라고요 → U19. -았/었었다 → U17.

steps.s: 한국어가 생각___ 재미있더라고요. (Korean was more fun than expected, I found.)
steps.hint: 생각보다 = more than expected.
예전에 = in the past / before / formerly.

steps.q: 오늘 정말 바빴어요. 생각보다 일이 많았어요. 퇴근하고 치킨을 시켰어요. How many past tense verbs?
steps.hint: 바빴어요, 많았어요, 시켰어요 = three past tense verbs.

steps.exampleEn: What do you think?
steps.note:
생각하다: to think.
생각 (thought/idea) + 하다 (do).

뭐라고 생각해요?: What do you think?
잘 생각해 보세요: Think about it carefully.

생각보다 = more than (I) thought.
생각이 나다 = to come to mind / to remember.

steps.exampleEn: Class starts. Class ended.
steps.note:
시작하다: to start/begin.
시작 (始作): 시 (begin) + 작 (make).

끝나다: to end/finish.
끝 (end) + 나다 (come out).

수업이 시작해요: class starts.
수업이 끝났어요: class ended.
시작합시다!: Let's start!

steps.exampleEn: I go outside. I enter inside.
steps.note:
나가다: to go out.
나 (out) + 가다 (go).

들어가다: to enter / go in.
들어 (into) + 가다 (go).

밖에 나가요: I go outside.
안에 들어가요: I go inside.
집에 들어가요: I go into the house.

나오다: to come out (나 + 오다).
들어오다: to come in (들어 + 오다).

steps.hint: 끝나다 = to end. 끝났어요 = ended (past).
나 = out. 나가다 = go out. 나오다 = come out.

steps.title: Turning Verbs Into Nouns — Preview
steps.desc:
Korean can turn any verb into a noun.

먹다 (to eat) → 먹는 것 (the act of eating / eating)

This is called nominalization.
Today is just a taste. Full treatment in Unit 18.
goals: Understand '~는 것 같아요' (I think / it seems)
Preview for full system in Unit 18

steps.exampleEn: I think it's raining. / It seems like it's raining.
steps.note:
~는 것 같아요: I think / it seems.

비가 오는 것 같아요.
오는: coming (verb → modifier)
것: thing
같다: to be like/seem

Literally: 'It's like the thing of rain coming.'
Meaning: 'It seems like it's raining.'

This is THE most common way to soften opinions in Korean.
Instead of 비가 와요 (it's raining — direct),
비가 오는 것 같아요 (it seems like it's raining — soft).

steps.en: I think it's fun / it seems fun
steps.exampleEn: I think this movie is fun.
steps.note:
재미있는 것 같아요.
재미있다 → 재미있는 (modifier)
것 같다 = seems like

More examples:
맛있는 것 같아요: I think it's delicious.
좋은 것 같아요: I think it's good.
어려운 것 같아요: I think it's difficult.

것 같아요 is a SOFTENER.
Korean speakers use it to avoid being too direct.

steps.exampleEn: Learning Korean is fun.
steps.note:
-기: turns a verb into a noun.

배우다 → 배우기: learning
먹다 → 먹기: eating
읽다 → 읽기: reading

한국어 배우기가 재미있어요.
= Learning Korean is fun.

-기 is used in:
- TOPIK sections: 읽기 (reading), 쓰기 (writing), 듣기 (listening)
- Daily phrases: 먹기 전에 (before eating)

⚡ JRTF: Full nominalization system in Unit 18.

steps.title: 💭 것 같아요 — The Korean Softener
steps.text:
Korean culture values indirectness.
것 같아요 is the tool for that.

비싸요. (It's expensive.) — direct, could be rude
비싼 것 같아요. (It seems expensive.) — soft, polite

좋아요. (It's good.) — direct
좋은 것 같아요. (I think it's good.) — soft

맛없어요. (It doesn't taste good.) — direct, very rude
맛없는 것 같아요. (I don't think it tastes good.) — softer

Native speakers use 것 같아요 constantly.
It's not weakness — it's social intelligence.

⚡ JRTF: Full nominalization in Unit 18.
For now: use 것 같아요 to soften opinions.

opts: I think it's raining / it seems like rain
steps.ans: I think it's raining / it seems like rain
steps.hint: ~는 것 같아요 = I think / it seems like.
-기 = nominalization. 배우기 = learning (noun).

steps.q: Why do Koreans use 것 같아요 so much?
steps.hint: 것 같아요 = indirect expression. Korean culture values softness.
읽다 → 읽기 = reading (noun). TOPIK 읽기 = reading section.

steps.s: 이 영화 재미있는 ___ 같아요. (I think this movie is fun.)
steps.hint: 것 같아요 = seems like / I think.

steps.s: 한국어 배우___가 재미있어요. (Learning Korean is fun.)
steps.hint: -기 = verb → noun. 배우기 = learning.

steps.s: 이 음식 {1} 것 같아요. 한국 음식 {2}가 재미있어요.
steps.hint: I think this food is delicious. Making Korean food is fun.

steps.q: This lesson is a preview for:
steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: Nominalization fully taught in Unit 18.

steps.q: 맛있는 것 같아요 is softer than 맛있어요 because:
steps.hint: 것 같아요 = seems/I think → indirectness = politeness.

steps.s: 이거 좋___ 것 같아요. (I think this is good.)
steps.hint: 좋다 (adj) + past/descriptive modifier 은 + 것 같아요.
듣다 → 듣기 = listening. TOPIK 듣기 = listening section.

steps.q: ~는 것 같아요 is used constantly in Korean because:
opts: It shows social intelligence and indirectness
steps.ans: It shows social intelligence and indirectness
steps.hint: Softening opinions = valued in Korean culture.

steps.title: Three Time Frames for Modifiers
steps.desc:
Unit 7 Lesson 8 showed you -는 (present modifier).

Now: all three time frames together.

먹는 음식: food I eat (present)
먹은 음식: food I ate (past)
먹을 음식: food I will eat (future)

Just recognize. Production comes in Unit 18.
goals: See all three modifier forms together

steps.example: 먹는 음식, 먹은 음식, 먹을 음식
steps.exampleEn: food I eat, food I ate, food I'll eat
steps.note:
Three modifier forms:

-는 (present): 먹는 음식 (food I eat)
-은/ㄴ (past): 먹은 음식 (food I ate)
-을/ㄹ (future): 먹을 음식 (food I'll eat)

All come BEFORE the noun.
The verb turns into an adjective that modifies the noun.

Consonant stems: 먹는/먹은/먹을
Vowel stems: 가는/간/갈

⚡ JRTF: Full production in Unit 18.
For now: just see all three side by side.

steps.exampleEn: The food I ate yesterday was delicious.
steps.note:
먹은 음식:
먹다 → 먹 + 은 = 먹은 (past modifier).

Consonant stem + 은.
Vowel stem + ㄴ: 가다 → 간, 보다 → 본.

어제 먹은 음식이 맛있었어요.
= The food I ate yesterday was delicious.

어제 간 카페: the café I went to yesterday.
지난주에 본 영화: the movie I saw last week.

steps.exampleEn: I'm preparing the food I'll eat tomorrow.
steps.note:
먹을 음식:
먹다 → 먹 + 을 = 먹을 (future modifier).

Consonant stem + 을.
Vowel stem + ㄹ: 가다 → 갈, 보다 → 볼.

This is the SAME form as the future tense base:
먹을 거예요 (will eat) → 먹을 (future modifier).

준비하다: to prepare.
준비 (準備): preparation.

steps.title: 📋 Modifier Summary
steps.text:
PRESENT (-는): happening now / habitual
매일 먹는 음식 (food I eat every day)
사는 곳 (place I live)
좋아하는 것 (things I like)

PAST (-은/ㄴ): already happened
어제 먹은 음식 (food I ate yesterday)
간 카페 (café I went to)
본 영화 (movie I watched)

FUTURE (-을/ㄹ): will happen
내일 먹을 음식 (food I'll eat tomorrow)
갈 곳 (place I'll go)
볼 영화 (movie I'll watch)

All three go BEFORE the noun.
Korean: [modifier] + noun
English: noun + [that/which clause]

⚡ Unit 18 teaches this fully.

steps.hint: -은/ㄴ = past modifier. 먹은 = ate.
-을/ㄹ = future modifier. 먹을 = will eat.
Vowel stem + ㄴ = past modifier. 가 + ㄴ = 간.
Vowel stem + ㄹ = future modifier. 보 + ㄹ = 볼.

steps.q: All modifier forms go ___ the noun:
steps.hint: Korean: [modifier] + noun. Always before.

steps.s: 어제 ___ 영화가 재미있었어요. (The movie I watched yesterday was fun.)
steps.hint: Past modifier: 보다 → 본.

steps.s: 내일 ___ 곳이 어디예요? (Where is the place you'll go tomorrow?)
steps.hint: Future modifier: 가다 → 갈.

steps.s: 어제 {1} 카페가 좋았어요. 매일 {2} 커피가 맛있어요. 내일 {3} 곳은 서울이에요.
steps.hint: The café I went to yesterday was nice. The coffee I drink daily is good. The place I'll go tomorrow is Seoul.

steps.q: These three modifier forms are fully taught in:
steps.hint: ⚡ Full modifier production system: Unit 18.

steps.q: 어제 본 영화 — 본 is the ___ modifier:
steps.hint: 보다 → 본 (past modifier). 어제 본 영화 = movie I saw yesterday.

steps.s: 내일 ___ 음식을 준비해요. (I'm preparing food to eat tomorrow.)
steps.hint: Future modifier: 먹다 → 먹을 (will eat).

steps.s: {1} 음식은 비빔밥이에요. 어제 {2} 음식은 삼겹살이에요. 내일 {3} 음식은 치킨이에요.
steps.hint: Food I like is bibimbap. Food I ate yesterday is samgyeopsal. Food I'll eat tomorrow is chicken.

steps.title: A2 Complete — Comprehensive Assessment
steps.desc:
You've completed all of A2.

Let's test everything: past, present, future, conditionals, ability, speech levels, irregulars, connectors, vocabulary, and culture.

This is your A2 graduation.
goals: Demonstrate mastery of all three tenses

steps.hint: 가: 갔어요 (past), 가요 (present), 갈 거예요 (future).
ㄷ→ㄹ: 듣→들 + 어요 = 들어요.
ㅂ→우: 덥→더우 + 었어요 = 더웠어요.
ㄹ drops before ㅂ: 살→사 + ㅂ니다 = 삽니다.
ㅡ drops. Previous ㅏ = bright → 아. 바빠요.
-(으)면 + advice = 아프면 쉬세요.

steps.q: 한국어를 ___ 수 있어요. (I can speak Korean.)
steps.hint: 하다 → 할 수 있다.
먹다: 먹어요 (해요체) → 먹습니다 (합쇼체) → 먹어 (반말).
-아/어서 cannot be followed by commands/suggestions.

steps.q: 그 사람은 가고 ___. (She wants to go.)
steps.hint: 3rd person → -고 싶어해요.

steps.s: 내일 친구를 ___ 거예요. (I will meet a friend tomorrow.)
steps.hint: 만나다 → 만날 거예요.

steps.s: ___면 병원에 가세요. (If sick, go to the hospital.)
steps.hint: 아프다 + 면 = 아프면.

steps.s: 월요일___ 금요일___ 일해요.
steps.hint: 부터 = from. First blank: 월요일부터.

steps.s: 무리하___ 마세요. (Don't overdo it.)
steps.hint: Stem + 지 마세요 = don't do.

steps.s: 어제 편의점에서 도시락을 {1}. 오늘 친구하고 치킨을 {2} 거예요. 내일은 집에서 {3} 거예요.
steps.hint: Yesterday bought lunchbox. Today will order chicken. Tomorrow will rest.

steps.title: 🏆 A2 졸업! Congratulations!
steps.text:
A2 COMPLETE. Here's what you can do:

TENSES: past (았/었/했), present (-아/어요), future (-(으)ㄹ 거예요)
IRREGULARS: ㄷ, ㅂ, ㄹ, 으 — all four families
GRAMMAR: conditional (-(으)면), ability (-(으)ㄹ 수 있다), negative command (-지 마세요)
SPEECH LEVELS: 해요체, 합쇼체, 반말
CONNECTORS: -고, -아/어서, -고 나서, -지만, 그래서, 그런데
VOCAB: health, daily routine, housing, 편의점, 배달
CULTURE: 찜질방, Korean healthcare, 방 culture, 전세/월세, 배달

SEEDS PLANTED for B1:
-는데, -거든요, -고 있다, -더라고요, -았/었었다, -기, -는 것

B1 BEGINS with Unit 11:
여행 vocabulary, experiential -아/어 봤어요,
progressive -고 있다 NAMED, and more.

정말 잘 했어요! 🎉

steps.q: How many irregular families have you learned?
steps.hint: U7: ㄷ, ㅂ. U9: ㄹ, 으. Four families total in A2.
